risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Two randomisation lists are computer generated by a statistician otherwise not involved in the study." Comment: This describes a truly random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomisation by an independent statistician), which is sufficient for low risk of selection bias in this domain.

Quote: "Randomisation by a third party not involved in the study using a computerised random-number generator." Comment: The report states that allocation was carried out by a third party not involved in the study, and that the randomisation lists were stored by regional coordinators with allocation information handed out only after definitive inclusion of a practice. This indicates a mechanism that prevented recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was an overt group exercise / CBT-style fall-prevention program delivered at an institution, while the control group received usual care. The report describes training and delivery of the intervention but does not indicate any blinding of participants or personnel; given the nature of the intervention, participants and instructors would necessarily know group assignment. Comment: Because outcomes include subjective measures such as fear of falling, quality of life, and self-reported falls, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence reporting and behavior, so performance bias is likely high.

Summary: The paper states that falls were recorded by participants in a daily fall diary and that at study visits outcomes such as TUG, chair-stand, balance tests, EuroQol, FES-I, and PAR were assessed, but it does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded to allocation. Falls were patient self-report, and the other outcomes included a mix of objective performance measures and subjective questionnaires. Comment: Because blinding of outcome assessment is not reported and the outcomes are mixed, detection bias cannot be ruled out confidently; however, the objective physical tests reduce concern for some outcomes while the self-reported questionnaires and fall diary remain potentially biasable.

Summary: The report indicates planned repeated follow-up and an intention-to-treat/full-analysis approach, with a procedure to contact participants if fall diaries were not returned and to obtain basic fall information if they stopped filling in the diary. The text states: "Analyses of the primary endpoint... will be performed on base of the full-analysis set (complete information regarding the primary outcome parameter). Within this population, all subjects are considered regardless to their actual compliance... (intention to treat principle)." It also says: "If the diary is not received within 10 days after the end of the documented month the participant is contacted by phone. In case that during follow-up some patients stop to fill in the diary they will be phoned to ask for basic information on falls". This suggests attrition/missing outcome data were actively minimized and handled in a way that makes bias unlikely.
